abstract:
This study investigated the influence of intestinal microbiota on the cerebral metabolome using germ-free (GF) and colonized (Ex-GF) mice. Utilizing capillary electrophoresis with time-of-flight mass spectrometry (CE-TOFMS), the researchers identified 196 low-molecular-weight metabolites in the brain, 38 of which showed significant differences in concentration between the two groups.

Notable findings included a twofold increase in dopamine levels in GF mice, while Ex-GF mice exhibited higher concentrations of pipecolic acid and tryptophan. Furthermore, the presence of gut bacteria appeared to significantly alter cerebral energy metabolism; Ex-GF mice showed lower concentrations of glycolysis intermediates, ATP, and NADH, suggesting an accelerated metabolic flux to meet higher energy demands compared to GF mice. While some metabolites were uniquely synthesized in the brain, others showed similar concentration ratios across the colon, plasma, and brain, indicating they may bypass the blood-brain barrier. These results demonstrate that the microbiota-gut-brain axis plays a critical role in regulating cerebral metabolism, with potential implications for neurodevelopment, cognitive function, and the management of neurological disorders.
